steps.title: What x-intercepts mean
steps.content: An $x$-intercept is a point on the graph where it crosses the $x$-axis, which means the $y$-coordinate is 0. If $(-5, 0)$ is an $x$-intercept, then the point $(-5, 0)$ is on the graph of $f$. Using function notation, this translates directly to $f(-5) = 0$.

steps.title: Test each answer choice
steps.content:
Now check which statement must be true based on what we know:
  • Choice A: $f(0) = -5$ — the value at $x = 0$ is not specified by the given intercepts
  • Choice B: $f(6) = -5$ — this contradicts the fact that $(6, 0)$ is an $x$-intercept, so $f(6) = 0$
  • Choice C: $f(-5) = 0$ — this matches exactly what the $x$-intercept at $(-5, 0)$ tells us
  • Choice D: $f(-5) = 6$ — this contradicts our finding that $f(-5) = 0$
That matches choice C — C is correct.
